projects.title: Robot Battle Arena
projects.description: Developed a versatile robot capable of operating in various modes including manual control, autonomous wall-following, and attack mode, highlighting adaptability in robotics programming and system design.
achievements: Microcontroller Programming and Integration: Demonstrated proficiency in programming and integrating multiple ESP32-C3 microcontrollers, implementing a master-client architecture using ESP-NOW protocol for efficient inter-processor communication

Sensor Integration and Control Systems: Developed and implemented autonomous navigation algorithms using Time-of-Flight (ToF) sensors and proportional control, showcasing skills in sensor integration and feedback control systems

Multi-Modal Robotics Design: Created a versatile robot capable of operating in various modes including manual control, autonomous wall-following, and attack mode, highlighting adaptability in robotics programming and system design

Web-Based Interface Development: Designed and implemented a web-based control interface hosted on the master microcontroller, demonstrating full-stack development skills combining embedded systems with web technologies

Mechatronic System Optimization: Iteratively improved the robot's mechanical and electrical design, transitioning from a minimal footprint to a more practical two-platform layout, showcasing skills in mechanical design, electrical integration, and system optimization
Two VL53L0X ToF sensors are integrated for distance measurement. The front sensor aids in obstacle detection, while the left sensor provides feedback for wall-following algorithms.
The master controls two DC motors using PWM signals with encoder feedback for precise speed regulation.
Servo Motor used as an attacking arm in the battle arena, to hit other robots.
A laser cut shield is used to defend the robot from enemy attack.
components: Two VL53L0X ToF sensors for distance measurement
Motor Control with PWM signals and encoder feedback
Servo Motor used as an attacking arm

projects.title: F1/10 Autonomous Racing
projects.description: Developing and optimizing an autonomous 1:10 scale Formula 1 race car using ROS2 and state-of-the-art autonomous driving techniques.
achievements: Implemented real-time SLAM on an F1Tenth racecar using LiDAR and odometry to build accurate occupancy grid maps of indoor and outdoor race environments, then generated and logged waypoint trajectories for the car to follow autonomously at high speed.

Developed and tuned a Pure Pursuit controller for smooth high-speed waypoint tracking and implemented Autonomous Emergency Braking (AEB) using LiDAR scan thresholding to prevent collisions — validated in both simulation (Gazebo/RViz) and real-world track conditions.

Implemented and compared sampling-based motion planners including RRT for global path planning and MPPI for real-time reactive obstacle avoidance, evaluating trade-offs between planning horizon, compute cost, and collision safety on embedded hardware.

Deployed the full autonomy stack on an NVIDIA Jetson, integrating SICK LiDAR and stereo camera perception with localization, planning, and control nodes in a modular ROS2 architecture — achieving reliable Sim2Real transfer from Gazebo to physical hardware.

Competed in an outdoor autonomous racing competition, achieving a podium finish through optimized waypoint tuning, aggressive but stable Pure Pursuit gains, and robust real-time obstacle handling under race conditions.

projects.title: Autonomous Search and Rescue
projects.description: Built a real-time autonomous navigation and human detection system on an F1Tenth racecar platform, deploying MPPI-based obstacle avoidance and YOLOv8 human detection entirely onboard a Jetson Orin for search-and-rescue in cluttered environments.

videos.caption: Search and Rescue using F1/10th racecar

achievements: Designed and deployed a full autonomy stack on an F1Tenth racecar running entirely onboard an NVIDIA Jetson Orin — integrating SICK LiDAR, RealSense RGB-D camera, particle filter localization, MPPI motion planning, and YOLOv8 detection into a modular ROS2 pipeline operating at 30Hz in real time.

Implemented MPPI (Model Predictive Path Integral) control from scratch, sampling 256 kinematically feasible trajectory rollouts per cycle and evaluating each with a custom cost function penalizing obstacle proximity, curvature, and goal distance — enabling reactive navigation through cluttered, partially unknown environments without a global obstacle map.

Engineered a geometry-aware rectangle footprint collision model to replace the naive point-mass abstraction, computing minimum clearance across all four vehicle corners at each timestep to produce sharper near-miss vs. collision discrimination and safer high-speed maneuvering.

Built a LiDAR preprocessing pipeline to handle noisy real-world sensor data — filtering NaN/Inf returns, rejecting low-intensity reflections from specular surfaces, and clipping range outliers — improving MPPI obstacle cost reliability in a real hallway environment.

Integrated YOLOv8 human detection with Intel RealSense depth alignment to localize detected persons as 3D world-frame coordinates using the pinhole camera model, publishing detections to a ROS2 topic for first-responder visualization on RViz at 86% peak confidence.

Validated the full system across 10 autonomous laps in a real cluttered environment with no prior obstacle knowledge, achieving 80% navigation success and 90% human detection accuracy — with failures traced to Jetson compute bottlenecks at dense LiDAR loads rather than algorithmic failures.

projects.title: Drone Racing using RL
projects.description: Trained a PPO policy in NVIDIA Isaac Lab to fly a quadcopter through a 7-gate race circuit, developing a full reward shaping pipeline, gate-crossing detection system, and domain randomization curriculum from scratch.

videos.caption: The final policy flying through the course
Training the policy in Isaac Lab

achievements: Designed a 27-dimensional world-frame observation space for a quadcopter racing agent — iterating from gate/body-frame encodings to world-frame gate-relative vectors (prev, current, next gate + approach normal + sin/cos lap phase), a switch that was the single most impactful improvement to training stability.

Built a direction-enforced gate crossing detector that simultaneously tracks all gates per timestep, checking sign-flip of the drone's x-coordinate in each gate frame within bounds — correctly penalizing wrong-gate and wrong-direction crossings that the naive single-gate check allowed the policy to exploit.

Designed and debugged a multi-term reward function through ~6 major iterations: identified and fixed a progress reward spike on crossing steps (distance computed against new gate vs. old baseline), a two-sided gate double-penalty bug, and an uninitialized prev_x reset bug — each of which silently degraded training.

Implemented a linear domain randomization curriculum over aerodynamic drag, thrust-to-weight ratio, and attitude PID gains — ramping randomization range from zero to full over a configurable iteration window so the policy learns gate traversal under stable dynamics before generalizing.

Achieved consistent 7-gate lap completion including correct traversal of a reverse-approach gate (Gate 3), after systematically isolating reward, observation, and reset bugs that had kept the policy stuck hovering in front of gates for several days of iteration.

projects.title: Semantic Segmentation for Autonomous Driving
projects.description: Implemented and compared CNN and transformer-based semantic segmentation architectures on urban driving scenes, evaluating trade-offs between model complexity, class imbalance handling, and real-world perception performance.
achievements: Project:

Implemented a U-Net architecture from scratch in PyTorch with a symmetric encoder-decoder design, Group Normalization, skip connections, and Dice loss to handle class imbalance, training end-to-end on the Cityscapes urban driving dataset for 50 epochs achieving 28% mIoU.

Built a custom PyTorch dataset wrapper with synchronized image-mask augmentation pipelines using Albumentations, including Gaussian noise, brightness/contrast variation, and horizontal flipping to simulate real-world sensor and lighting variability.

Fine-tuned a pretrained SegFormer-B1 transformer model on Cityscapes by freezing the hierarchical encoder and retraining only the MLP decoder with AdamW and weighted cross-entropy loss, boosting mIoU from 55% to 65% and significantly improving segmentation of safety-critical classes like pedestrians, traffic signs, and poles.

Performed detailed per-class IoU analysis across 19 semantic categories, identifying that transformer-based global attention is critical for small and rare object detection — SegFormer achieved 70% IoU on trucks and buses where U-Net scored zero.

Analyzed architectural trade-offs between convolutional and transformer-based models for real-time robotic perception, concluding that loss function design and class weighting are as impactful as architecture choice for deployment on class-imbalanced real-world datasets.

projects.title: Neural Radiance Field (NeRF) for 3D Scene Reconstruction
projects.description: Implemented a simplified NeRF pipeline from scratch, training a neural network to represent a 3D scene as a continuous volumetric radiance field and synthesize novel viewpoints from a sparse set of input images.
achievements: Project: https://github.com/neelmulayPenn/TinyNeRF

Implemented the full NeRF pipeline from scratch in PyTorch including ray generation, stratified depth sampling, sinusoidal positional encoding, and differentiable volume rendering with alpha compositing, training end-to-end on 100 LEGO images captured from diverse viewpoints.

Ran COLMAP structure-from-motion to estimate camera intrinsics and extrinsics from raw images, successfully registering 99/100 cameras into a sparse 3D reconstruction, then converted poses to the NeRF coordinate convention using colmap2nerf.

Implemented volume rendering from first principles using the physically-based transmittance model — computing per-sample opacity, cumulative transmittance, and weighted color compositing — enabling differentiable gradient flow through the entire rendering pipeline.

Trained a 4-layer MLP with 128 hidden dimensions and 63-dimensional positional encoding on a Google Colab L4 GPU for 5000 iterations, reducing MSE loss from 0.18 to 0.004 and producing recognizable novel view synthesis of the LEGO bulldozer from multiple viewpoints.

Analyzed failure modes including depth sampling range mismatches at extreme viewing angles and blurring from the simplified view-independent radiance model, identifying hierarchical sampling and view-dependent color as key extensions needed for production-quality NeRF.

projects.title: Vehicle Lane Change with MPC
projects.description: Built a full-stack autonomous driving framework for structured road environments, integrating a 6-state dynamic bicycle model, hierarchical behavior planning with TTC-based lane-change logic, quintic trajectory generation, and linearized Model Predictive Control solved with Drake + SNOPT. Validated across 5 traffic scenarios including overtaking, highway merge, and 4-way intersection navigation.

videos.caption: Vehicle Lane Change due to a stalled vehicle in the lane
Vehicle Lane Change: Merging into a highway

achievements: Project: https://github.com/neelmulayPenn/VehicleLaneChange

Implemented a 6-state dynamic bicycle model (X, Y, ψ, vx, vy, ω) with front/rear slip-angle lateral forces, numerically linearized around reference operating points and Euler-discretized for use as the MPC prediction model — enabling both high-fidelity simulation rollout and tractable constrained optimization.

Designed a hierarchical behavior-trajectory-control stack: a finite-state behavior planner with TTC hazard detection, hysteresis, and cooldown logic feeds quintic C²-continuous lane-change profiles to a linearized MPC, decoupling behavioral decision-making from real-time trajectory optimization.

Formulated and solved a finite-horizon constrained QP at each timestep using Drake's MathematicalProgram + SNOPT (T=15, Δt=0.1s), with separate Q, Qf, R, and Rd weights penalizing tracking error, control effort, and control rate of change to enforce actuator smoothness.

Stress-tested the closed-loop stack under realistic perception conditions — 5 Hz asynchronous updates with Gaussian position noise — achieving a 96.7% success rate (29/30 trials) across 1–6 obstacle vehicles in straight-road overtake scenarios.

Identified and documented root-cause failure modes across 5 scenarios: gap persistence filtering needed for impassable traffic, curvature-aware feedforward required for curved roads, and a dedicated yield/stop primitive needed for intersection handling — all grounded in control-theoretic analysis.

projects.title: Pick and Place with Robot Arm
projects.description: Implemented inverse kinematics algorithms for precise control of a 7-DOF robotic arm, enabling accurate block detection, pick-up, and stacking in both static and dynamic environments.
achievements: Advanced Robotic Manipulation: Implemented inverse kinematics (IK) algorithms for precise control of a robotic arm, enabling accurate block detection, pick-up, and stacking in both static and dynamic environments
Computer Vision Integration: Utilized camera feedback and April tag detection to locate and orient blocks, demonstrating proficiency in integrating vision systems with robotic control

Dynamic Object Interaction: Developed two approaches for interacting with moving objects - a sweep approach and a prediction-based method - showcasing adaptability in handling real-time, dynamic scenarios
Multi-threaded Programming: Implemented a multithreading approach for concurrent computation of inverse kinematics solutions, significantly improving system efficiency and responsiveness

Hardware-Software Integration: Successfully translated simulation results to real hardware, overcoming challenges related to sensor discrepancies, environmental variations, and mechanical limitations through iterative testing and calibration

projects.title: Multi-Agent Drone Formation Control
projects.description: Built a modular, three‑phase control architecture for coordinating teams of robots to form shapes, follow a moving leader, and dynamically reshape for coverage or obstacle accommodation. Phase I uses the Hungarian algorithm for globally optimal waypoint assignment into arbitrary planar formations. Phase II transitions to a leader–follower controller that uses velocity feedforward, spring–damper error correction, and short‑range repulsion with only leader broadcasts and local neighbor sensing—no all‑to‑all comms. Phase III extends the controller with a leader‑triggered expansion factor γ to scale formation radius, increasing coverage while preserving safety. The design mirrors real deployments: a global initialization window, followed by decentralized tracking robust to partial information and link loss.

videos.caption: Drone Formation - Switching controllers in an 'obstacle zone'
Drone Formation - Drones entering the formation via the Hungarian algorithm, and avoiding collision

achievements: Implemented three-phase formation control framework for 15 agents achieving convergence in under 12 seconds.
Developed decentralized leader-follower controller with MSE tracking error under 0.04 m².
Integrated Hungarian algorithm for optimal agent-waypoint assignment preventing conflicts.
Built collision avoidance system using short-range repulsion fields ensuring agent safety.
Created adaptive formation scaling achieving 50% coverage increase within 2 seconds on-demand.
Link to work: https://drive.google.com/file/d/1Z3gihs5Ecm0Sc0oSaJTWsMsB9F11QiZi/view?usp=sharing

projects.title: Fast Car
projects.description: Constructed a robot using a 1/4 inch MDF panel base with DC motors, integrated encoders, and wheels.
achievements: Developed a circuit using an ESP32C3 microcontroller, H-bridge motor driver, and various connectors
Created a WiFi-based control system with a custom web interface for remote operation
Implemented PID control for precise speed management of each motor
Won first place in the speed competition by completing 3 laps around the obstacle

projects.title: Dancing Man
projects.description: Designed and implemented a 3-DOF interactive input and output device that mimics a dancer.
achievements: Developed an ADC-based potentiometer control system for precise servo movement
Created a custom CAD design for the dancer's structure
Fabricated components using laser-cutting techniques
Implemented a system where the input device controls the output dancer's movements
Integrated three servo motors to control the dancer's arms and legs independently
Programmed the microcontroller to interpret potentiometer inputs and translate them to servo outputs
Achieved smooth and accurate replication of input movements on the output dancer
Won prize as the best dancer in class

projects.title: Sensors Lab
projects.description: Designed and implemented various digital logic and sensing systems, showcasing skills in microcontroller programming, circuit design, and signal processing.
achievements: Developed a debounced switch interface using an ATmega32U4 microcontroller, implementing both hardware (RC filter) and software debouncing techniques
Created a phototransistor-based light sensing circuit, optimizing sensitivity for hand gesture detection at 20+ cm distance
Designed and built an IR frequency detection system capable of distinguishing between 25 Hz and 662 Hz signals
Implemented advanced signal processing techniques to detect weak IR signals (662 Hz) in the presence of ambient light noise at distances over 1 meter
Utilized oscilloscopes, function generators, and various electronic components to analyze and troubleshoot circuit behavior

projects.title: Trajectory Optimization for 7-DOF Arm
projects.description: Implemented and compared two path planning algorithms for a robotic arm: Rapidly-exploring Random Trees (RRT) and Potential Field methods.
achievements: Developed a bidirectional RRT algorithm that significantly improved convergence time compared to unidirectional RRT
Implemented a potential field planner with attractive and repulsive forces for goal-seeking and obstacle avoidance
Created forward kinematics and Jacobian calculations for a 7-joint robotic arm with additional virtual joints
Designed collision detection algorithms for both planners
Conducted extensive testing on various obstacle configurations and analyzed performance metrics
The RRT planner demonstrated superior performance, successfully finding collision-free paths in 80% of trials for simple environments and 50% for more complex scenarios
Average planning times ranged from 0.9 to 2.9 seconds depending on environment complexity

projects.title: SMA Characterization of NiTi wires (Thesis)
projects.description: Conducted an in-depth study on the thermomechanical properties of Nickel-Titanium (NiTi) shape memory alloy wires for actuator applications.
achievements: Performed thermomechanical characterization of thin NiTi wires using various testing methods
Investigated the impact of microstructure changes on the fundamental properties and functional behavior of NiTi wires
Conducted tensile tests till fracture and cyclic tests at different temperatures (20°C, 90°C, 120°C, and 150°C)
Analyzed the relationship between annealing time, microstructure, and mechanical properties such as ultimate tensile stress, plateau stress, ductility, and plateau strain
Implemented superelastic cycling tests to evaluate the strain recovery and permanent deformation characteristics
Performed thermal cyclic tests to study the shape memory effect under various constant stress levels
Achieved a 60% enhancement in ductility and a 25% increase in actuator response speed through optimized heat treatment
projects.location: Institute of Physics, Czech Academy of Sciences, Prague, under the supervision of Dr. Petr Sittner and Dr. Lukas Kadevarek

projects.title: Composite Analysis & Modeling
projects.description: Conducted an analysis of temperature-dependent properties of fiber-reinforced composites.
achievements: Developed two predictive models (Model I and Model II) to simulate the temperature-dependent ultimate tensile strength (UTS) of fiber-reinforced polymer composites
Model I utilized the Halpin-Tsai equation to predict UTS based on elastic modulus, accounting for physical and thermal properties
Model II incorporated a probabilistic approach using the Weibull distribution to address variability in fiber strength due to temperature fluctuations
Implemented complex mathematical models in MATLAB to perform simulations across temperature ranges
Conducted experimental validation through standardized tensile testing and matrix digestion tests
Analyzed how factors like fiber volume fraction, fiber/matrix properties, and temperature affect composite strength
